_comment: Hand-written by Claude for the 4 templated junk gambit files. Replaces only the main-line moveAnnotations[i].annotation; subLines untouched. Voice matches the hand-curated kings-gambit / evans-gambit / budapest-gambit / benko-gambit files in repertoire.
gambit-kings-gambit: This is the King's Gambit, one of the oldest and most romantic openings in chess — White claims the center with 1.e4 and prepares the audacious f4 sacrifice that has produced four centuries of brilliant attacking games.
Black accepts the central challenge with e5, the principled response that anchors the e-file and sets up the King's Gambit's defining tension.
f4 — the King's Gambit pawn sacrifice. White offers the f-pawn to rip open the f-file, dismantle Black's e5 anchor, and prepare a lightning attack on f7 before Black's pieces find their squares.

Black takes the gambit pawn — the King's Gambit Accepted is on the board. White is materially down but has a half-open f-file, a development lead, and the kind of initiative that has destroyed unwary defenders since Greco's day.
Nf3 develops with tempo and prevents the venomous ...Qh4+, which would otherwise wreck White's coordination. Without this prophylactic move, the Gambit collapses on move three.
g5 — the classical defense. Black anchors the f4 pawn with g5, daring White to either chase the material or commit to the wild Kieseritzky-style main lines.
Bc4 develops aiming directly at f7, the eternal weakness in Black's position. Every White piece in the King's Gambit gets a job; this one's job is the Black king.
Bg7 fianchettoes the dark-squared bishop, defending h6 and the long diagonal while preparing to weather the storm Black knows is coming.
h4 — the famous Kieseritzky lever. White attacks the g5 pawn and forces Black into either ...g4 (giving up the f4 anchor) or ...h6 (passive defense). The real fireworks start now.
g4 — Black goes all-in on holding the gambit pawn, kicking the f3 knight and accepting that the position will become wildly tactical. The classical King's Gambit fight is fully joined.
Ne5 — the Kieseritzky knight leap. White ignores the kick and lands on the powerful e5 outpost, eyeing f7, g4, and d7 simultaneously while preparing Nxg4 to recover material.
Qe7 covers e5 and e4 from the same square, prepares ...Nbc6 to challenge the white knight, and keeps Black's setup theoretically sound despite the cramped piece placement.
d4 grabs the rest of the center. White's d4-e4 pawn duo supports the e5 knight, locks the position in White's favor, and prepares Nbc3 for full development.
d6 challenges the e5 knight and frees Black's pieces. The position is becoming a strategic battle — White has the center and development, Black has the extra pawn and the bishop pair.
Nd3 retreats actively — the knight aims for the f4 pawn, eyes b4 and e5, and clears the e-file for the queen. Every White piece is repositioned with the gambit pawn in its sights.
Nf6 develops the last minor piece, attacks e4, and prepares ...O-O. With every piece on a meaningful square, Black has solved development; the question is whether the extra pawn survives.
Nxf4 — White recovers the gambit pawn at last. The knight on f4 attacks d5, controls e6 and h5, and stares at f7. The material is even, but White's initiative is concrete and ongoing.
Nxe4 — Black grabs the e4 pawn, daring White to prove the compensation. A critical moment: White must justify the second pawn investment with rapid attack or accept a structurally inferior endgame.
O-O brings the king to safety and the rook to f1, joining the attack along the half-open f-file. With the king in the center, White's whole investment would unravel; castling is non-negotiable.
d5 — Black gains central space and shields the king from White's threats. The extra pawn becomes meaningful only if Black survives the next ten moves of pressure.
Bb3 retreats to dodge ...d4 next while keeping the bishop's diagonal aimed at f7. Quiet, precise — White is reorganizing before the next storm.
Nc6 develops with pressure on d4 and prepares ...Nb4 or ...O-O-O. Every Black piece has finally found a meaningful square.
Re1 swings the rook into the e-file battery, eyeing the e4 knight and supporting any future Nf4-d5 ideas. White's coordination reaches its peak.
Bf5 develops Black's last piece, defends the e4 knight from Re1's pressure, and challenges White's coordination. The position is fully loaded.
Nc3 attacks the e4 knight, forcing concrete decisions. Black must trade, retreat, or escalate — White is asking the question.
Nxc3 simplifies the central tension. The trade relieves immediate pressure but commits Black to facing the structural consequences.
bxc3 — White's pawn structure shifts. The doubled c-pawns are ugly but the b-file opens for the rook, and the dark-square diagonal becomes thematic for the c1-bishop.
O-O-O — Black castles queenside, signaling commitment to a counterattack on the kingside. Opposite-side castling: the position becomes a race, and tempo is everything.
Ba3 — the killer move. White's bishop targets the a3-f8 diagonal, threatens to dismantle Black's queenside structure, and prepares c4 to crack open the lines toward the Black king.
Qf6 protects the bishop and prepares ...Qg6 or ...Qh4 for kingside counterplay. Critical position — both kings exposed, both sides loaded for action.
gambit-evans-gambit: This is the Evans Gambit, one of the most romantic and aggressive openings in chess history — Capablanca called it 'the gem of openings.' White plays 1.e4, claiming the center and preparing for a pawn sacrifice that yields rapid development and a dangerous attack.
Black responds with e5, claiming central space and entering the classical Italian and Open Game complex.
Nf3 develops with attack on e5 and prepares the Italian setup. The knight will be a key attacking piece if Black accepts the gambit.
Nc6 defends e5 and develops with tempo — the classical answer to the Italian.
Bc4 — the Italian Game's defining move. The bishop targets the eternally weak f7 square and joins the kingside attack the Evans Gambit will soon unleash.
Bc5 mirrors White's development and challenges the central squares. Black is in the Giuoco Piano — and walking right into the Evans Gambit's setup.

b4 — the Evans Gambit pawn sacrifice. White offers the b-pawn to gain a tempo on the c5 bishop and prepare c3-d4 with a powerful pawn center. Captain Evans's idea from 1827 still ranks among the most ambitious gambits ever conceived.
Bxb4 accepts the gambit. Black is up a pawn but White will spend the next several moves extracting maximum compensation — central pawns, development tempo, and attacking chances.
c3 attacks the b4 bishop and prepares the all-important d4 advance. White is building the dream Evans pawn center while Black scrambles for safe bishop placement.
Ba5 — the main-line Evans response. The bishop maintains pressure on the a5-e1 diagonal (pinning the c3 pawn against potential checks) and refuses to give White's center an easy ride.
d4 — the central thrust. White builds the powerful e4-d4 pawn duo, attacks the e5 pawn, and threatens to roll the center forward with overwhelming force.
exd4 captures the pawn. Black accepts material but White will recapture with the c-pawn and emerge with a massive center plus a development lead.
O-O brings the king to safety before the central tension resolves. White is happy to let exd4 sit on the board for a moment — castling first is more important than recapturing.
d6 supports the d4 pawn and prepares ...Nf6 development. Black is trying to consolidate the extra pawn before White's center steamrolls.
cxd4 — White recaptures, and the classical Evans Gambit pawn center is born: e4 and d4 pawns side by side, controlling every key central square.
Bb6 retreats the bishop to a safer square and adds pressure on d4. Black's bishop is now eyeing f2, the eternal weak spot in White's castled position.
d5 — the central pawn lever. White pushes the c-knight back to a5 (a passive square) and gains crushing central space.
Na5 — the c6 knight is forced to the rim where it's poorly placed. Black accepts the awkward retreat to dodge worse alternatives like ...Nb8.
Bb2 — the long-diagonal bishop. The Bb2 stares directly at Black's king on g8 along the a1-h8 diagonal, building the classic Evans attack on the dark squares.
Ne7 develops the kingside knight while sidestepping the d5 pawn's pressure. Black is desperately trying to complete development.
Bd3 redeploys the bishop where it can support the kingside attack and avoid being kicked by ...c6 or ...c5. The Bd3 lines up with Bb2 to form the classic Evans attacking battery.
O-O — Black finally gets the king to safety, but the kingside is under siege. White's pieces are coordinated for an attack; Black's are scrambling to defend.
Nc3 develops the queenside knight and joins the central pressure. White's army is now fully mobilized for the attack on Black's king.
Ng6 redeploys the e7 knight to a more active square, defending the kingside and preparing ...f5 if needed.
Ne2 — the queen knight reroutes toward the kingside. White is patient: every piece must be on its optimal attacking square before the assault begins.

c5 — Black strikes back at White's center, looking for counterplay and trying to relieve the queenside pressure. The position is now a complex strategic battle, the gambit pawn long since transformed into something else.
gambit-budapest-gambit: This is the Budapest Gambit, a surprise weapon where Black sacrifices the e-pawn for rapid piece development and trappy counterplay. White opens with d4, claiming the center.
Nf6 — the standard Indian response. Black develops the knight and contests the central squares without committing to a pawn structure yet.
c4 expands White's central footprint and prepares the classical Queen's Pawn setup. With the c-pawn out, White invites Black to choose a defense.

e5 — the Budapest Gambit. Black sacrifices the e-pawn to disrupt White's smooth development and create immediate tactical problems with ...Ng4 ideas. Adler's gambit from 1896 still rattles unprepared 1.d4 players.
dxe5 takes the bait. White grabs the gambit pawn, knowing the e5 pawn will likely come back to Black's pieces — but extracting maximum compensation along the way is the goal.
Ng4 — the Budapest's signature move. The knight leaps to g4 to attack the e5 pawn and threatens ...Nxe5 with full development. The Budapest's tactical engine is revving.
Bf4 — the principled defense. The bishop develops actively and protects the e5 pawn against ...Nxe5, neutralizing Black's primary tactical idea.
Nc6 develops with pressure on e5 and prepares ...Bb4+. Black is generating threats on every move — that's the Budapest dream.
Nf3 develops the kingside knight, defending the e5 pawn a third time and preparing castling. White's position is becoming solid.
Bb4+ — the Rubinstein-style check. The bishop pin and check force White to address the king before completing development, and prepare ...Bxc3 to wreck the white pawn structure.
Nbd2 blocks the check and develops the queenside knight. White avoids ...Bxc3 doubling and keeps the position structurally sound.
Qe7 prepares ...Ngxe5 — winning the gambit pawn back since the f4 bishop now blocks the queen's defensive line — and contests the e-file.
a3 prepares to challenge the Bb4 with b4 next, forcing Black to commit to either trading bishops or retreating with ...Ba5.
Ngxe5 — Black recovers the gambit pawn at last. The double knight recapture is the cleanest way to dismantle White's defensive setup.
Nxe5 — White trades knights to clarify the position. The exchange relieves some tension and sets up White's strategic plans.
Nxe5 recaptures on e5, completing Black's development and equalizing material. The Budapest gamble has paid off — Black has full development, no weaknesses, and a roughly equal middlegame.
e3 supports d4 (if White plays it later), prepares Bd3, and consolidates White's structure. White accepts the equalized position and plays for small structural advantages.
Bxd2+ — Black trades the dark-squared bishop to relieve tactical pressure. The bishop's job is done; better to simplify than allow White to chase it with b4.
Qxd2 — White recaptures with the queen and centralizes. The queen on d2 is well-placed for both kingside and queenside operations.
d6 supports the e5 knight and prepares ...Bf5 for kingside development. Black's setup is solid and flexible.
Be2 develops modestly and prepares castling. White's bishop will find better squares later; for now, getting the king to safety matters most.
O-O — Black tucks the king to safety. With every piece on a useful square, the opening phase is essentially over.
O-O — White also castles. With both kings safe and pieces developed, the game enters the strategic middlegame phase.
Bf5 develops the last bishop to an active square, eyeing the b1-h7 diagonal and pressuring White's kingside coordination.
b4 — White expands on the queenside, targeting the c5 square and preparing pawn breaks. The queenside becomes the primary battlefield.
a5 — Black challenges the queenside expansion immediately. The pawn lever gives Black counter-chances on the queenside files.
Rab1 — White lifts the rook to support the b-file pressure and prepare any necessary defense or expansion. The rook joins the queenside operation.
Rfe8 — the kingside rook swings to e8 to control the e-file. With Black's pieces coordinated, the position is genuinely balanced.
Rfc1 — White's other rook joins the c-file, eyeing c5 and any tactics involving Black's queenside structure.
axb4 — Black captures to clarify the queenside structure. The trade simplifies White's attacking ambitions.
axb4 recaptures and the queenside pawns are halved. The endgame is approaching, with both sides having equal material and clear strategic plans.
gambit-benko-gambit: This is the Benko Gambit, one of the most respected and dynamic gambits in modern chess — Black sacrifices a pawn on the queenside for lasting pressure on the a-, b-, and c-files that often endures into the endgame. White opens with d4, claiming central space.
Nf6 — the standard Indian response, contesting the center without committing to a pawn structure. Black is keeping every option open.
c4 expands White's central footprint. With both central pawns out, White invites the Benoni and Benko complex.
c5 — Black challenges the central setup. The c5 pawn locks horns with d4 and signals that Black wants asymmetrical, double-edged play.
d5 — White declines the central trade and locks the position. The d5 pawn becomes the central reference point for both sides' strategy.

b5 — the Benko Gambit. Black sacrifices the b-pawn to pry open the queenside files and create lasting pressure that endures deep into the endgame. Pal Benko's signature contribution to opening theory.
cxb5 takes the gambit pawn. White accepts the material, knowing the queenside will be opened and pressure will follow — but the pawn itself is real.
a6 — the second offer. Black insists on opening the a-file for the rook, refusing to back down from the gambit's strategic premise.
bxa6 — White accepts both pawns. The Fianchetto Variation lets Black push for activity, but the material count favors White by two pawns — a meaningful margin if Black's pressure dissipates.
Bxa6 — Black recaptures with the bishop, immediately activating it on the a6-f1 diagonal. The Benko's positional foundation: bishop pair, open queenside, lasting pressure.
Nc3 develops the queenside knight and prepares e4 for the central buildup. White is methodically organizing despite the unusual pawn imbalance.
d6 reinforces the central pawn and prepares ...Nbd7. The Benko's strategic motif is now in place: solid center, active queenside, dark-squared bishop heading to g7.
e4 — White grabs maximum central space. The Benko's classical antidote: build a massive central pawn structure that compensates for the queenside weakness.

Bxf1 — Black trades the a6 bishop for White's f1 bishop. The exchange forces White to recapture with the king — no other piece can reach f1 — permanently denying queenside castling and weakening the white king's home.
Kxf1 — White recaptures with the king. The king on f1 is awkward but the position is structurally sound; the king will tuck to g2 in a moment.
g6 prepares the fianchetto of the dark-squared bishop. The Bg7 will sit on the long a1-h8 diagonal, exerting pressure that complements the open queenside files.
g3 prepares Kg2 — the king walks to safer ground after the forced Kxf1, and the king walk also makes Re1 a coherent followup.
Bg7 — the Benko fianchetto. The bishop sits on the long a1-h8 diagonal, pressuring White's center and supporting all of Black's queenside operations.
Kg2 — the king walks to safety. White's king is now reasonably placed, though the missing light-squared bishop creates long-term vulnerabilities on the kingside light squares.
O-O brings Black's king to safety. With both kings tucked away, the strategic battle for the queenside files begins in earnest.
Nf3 finally develops the kingside knight, supporting the central pawn structure and preparing Re1 for the e-file.
Nbd7 develops the queenside knight, preparing ...Qa5 and ...Rfb8 to mass forces on the open queenside files.
Re1 places the rook on the e-file, supporting any future e5 advance and maintaining White's central footprint.
Qa5 — the queen joins the queenside attack. From a5, the queen pressures c3, eyes the open a-file, and supports ...Nb6-c4 ideas.
Qc2 develops the queen and connects the rooks. White's queen also defends c3 and prepares to swing into action wherever needed.
Rfb8 — the king's rook swings to b8, completing the Benko's classical setup: rooks on a8 and b8 mass forces on the queenside.
Rb1 — White's rook contests the b-file. The defensive piece shuffle is in full swing as both sides organize for the strategic battle.
Nb6 — the knight redeploys to b6 where it eyes c4 and a4, preparing further pressure on the white queenside.
Nd2 — the f3 knight retreats to d2, defending against ...Nb6-c4 and rerouting toward b3 or f1 for queenside duty.
Nc4 — the knight lands on the powerful c4 outpost, attacking the white queen's knight on d2 and dominating the queenside dark squares.
Nxc4 — White trades the strong knight to relieve pressure. The exchange simplifies but Black still has lasting compensation for the gambit pawn.
Qxc3 — Black recoups material by capturing the c3 knight. The position has clarified: roughly equal material, Black has the Benko's structural compensation, and the endgame approaches.
